ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for straw mushroom as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for individual mushroom species as food is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw straw mushrooms is approximately 108 g (about 1 cup of whole mushrooms), providing roughly 29 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Straw mushroom, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron) taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for straw mushroom, raw (F14030008). Saturated fat, sugar, and additional micronutrient values (vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, water) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for Mushrooms, straw, raw (FDC ID 169403) where HPB does not report these fields. vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, and selenium_mcg are not reported in either HPB Foodo (F14030008) or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169403) for raw straw mushroom and are therefore set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Volvariella volvacea (straw mushroom) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 28 is estimated by analogy with button mushroom (Agaricus bisporus) and oyster mushroom, both of which are reported in the range 10–30 in peer-reviewed GI literature (Atkinson et al., 2008; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283). Straw mushrooms share a similar macronutrient profile (very low carbohydrate, high fibre, high protein relative to carbohydrate) and are expected to have an equivalent low GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (28 × 2.09 × 1.08) / 100 ≈ 0.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (21 mg/100 g) and high potassium (317 mg/100 g) yielding a favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Raw straw mushrooms are highly suitable for hypertension management; their sodium content of 21 mg/100 g and potassium content of 317 mg/100 g provide an Na:K molar ratio well below 1.0, consistent with DASH dietary targets for blood pressure reduction — consume freely as part of a low-sodium dietary pattern and avoid adding salt or high-sodium sauces during preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low net carbohydrate (2.09 g/100 g), high dietary fibre (2.1 g/100 g), and estimated low GI (~28)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw straw mushrooms have negligible glycaemic impact due to their very low carbohydrate content of 2.09 g/100 g, high dietary fibre of 2.1 g/100 g, and estimated GI of approximately 28; they can be consumed freely by individuals with type 2 diabetes without significant postprandial glucose elevation, provided they are not prepared with high-sugar sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.03 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high potassium, and beta-glucan dietary fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw straw mushrooms are cardioprotective; their saturated fat content of 0.03 g/100 g and zero cholesterol pose no atherogenic risk, while their beta-glucan-rich dietary fibre (2.1 g/100 g) and high potassium (317 mg/100 g) actively support LDL reduction and vascular health in line with AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beta-glucan content with immunomodulatory properties and negligible pro-inflammatory saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Straw mushrooms contain beta-glucans that may modulate immune activity relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; their negligible saturated fat and low sodium support an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern, and they should be thoroughly cooked before consumption to deactivate heat-labile compounds.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Straw mushroom (Volvariella volvacea), consumed raw, is a small to medium-sized edible fungus native to Southeast Asia and Southern China, widely cultivated on rice straw beds. The raw mushroom presents as a brown egg-shaped cap encased in a volva (outer membrane) when immature, with a firm, slightly slippery texture and a mild, earthy, subtly sweet flavour. It is a staple ingredient in Chinese, Thai, Vietnamese, and broader Southeast Asian cuisines, commonly used in stir-fries, soups, hotpots, and braised dishes. Nutritionally, raw straw mushrooms are very low in calories (27 kcal/100 g) and carbohydrates (2.09 g/100 g), with a notable protein content of 3.19 g/100 g for a vegetable, high dietary fibre at 2.1 g/100 g, and an excellent potassium-to-sodium ratio (317 mg K vs 21 mg Na per 100 g). Saturated fat is negligible at 0.03 g/100 g. The mushroom is a source of B vitamins including niacin (4.96 mg/100 g), folate (38 mcg/100 g), and riboflavin. Beta-glucan polysaccharides in the cell walls contribute to immunomodulatory and cholesterol-lowering effects documented in clinical literature. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 28 (low), making straw mushrooms suitable for glycaemic management. They are naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. Clinically, they are appropriate for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular disease.
common_user_queries: Are straw mushrooms good for diabetics?
Straw mushroom for high blood pressure
Straw mushroom vs button mushroom nutrition

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F14030008), an approved primary regional source, with confidence 0.95. Secondary micronutrient values (saturated fat, sugar, vitamins, minerals) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for Mushrooms, straw, raw (FDC ID 169403) where HPB does not report these fields. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008) as no direct measurement exists for Volvariella volvacea.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g is reported as 0.0 and trans_fat_g as 0.0 per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169403); these are confirmed trace/negligible values. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; mushrooms contain no dietary cholesterol. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 and vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; straw mushrooms contain no preformed vitamin A or B12. vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported for raw straw mushroom in either the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F14030008) or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169403) as of April 2026; both approved sources were consulted and neither provides values for these fields for this food item. GI value of 28 is an analogous estimate based on comparable low-carbohydrate mushroom species (button mushroom, oyster mushroom) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008); no direct GI measurement for Volvariella volvacea was found in approved sources. Saturated fat, sugar, and micronutrient values supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169403) where HPB does not report these fields; this is documented in nutrient_source. The hindi alias uses Devanagari script: स्ट्रॉ मशरूम, कच्चा.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Documented vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, and selenium_mcg as -1 values with reason and source in the 'notes' field to address G3 numeric hygiene failure from iteration 1.

Iteration 3: Expanded G3 documentation in 'notes' to explicitly state that both HPB Foodo (F14030008) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169403) were consulted and neither reports vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, or selenium_mcg for raw straw mushroom, satisfying the missing-data basis requirement for all three -1 sentinel values. Also added this documentation to nutrient_source.source_match_notes to ensure the basis is captured in the provenance record.
